coredata.dc:title: Effectively detect enoxaparin using polymyxin B as medium through fluorescent strategy based on carbon dots with antioxidant activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Liang
Cai, Rouying
Wang, Yao
Miao, Chenfang
Zhang, Menghan
Zheng, Chen
Zhang, Jianzhong
Huang, Zhengjun
Weng, Shaohuang
coredata.dc:description: Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) plays an important role in the prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolism. Sensitive and facile monitoring of LMWH is of great significance for clinical special populations. Herein, using enoxaparin (Enox) as the typical representative of LMWH, a convenient and accurate fluorescence method was developed for Enox detection using carbon dots with antioxidant activity (A-CDs) as probe and polymyxin B (PMB) as medium agent. Compared with the simple Enox or PMB, the formed complex of PMB and Enox (PMB-Enox) based on the electrostatic interaction exhibited a new absorbance peak at 340 nm. Correspondingly, the absorbance of PMB-Enox overlapped with the exciting wavelength of A-CDs at 350 nm and effectively quenched the fluorescence of A-CDs through inner filter effect (IFE). The decreased fluorescence intensities of A-CDs were controlled by the increased Enox with constant PMB. A linear fitting equation was obtained between the quenching efficiency (QE) of A-CDs and variable contents of Enox in the range of 0.8–25.0 μg/mL. And the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.64 μg/mL. The recovery of the injection of Enox in human plasma was acceptable with rational relative standard deviations. Moreover, this fluorescence sensing strategy possesses the prospect of developing reliable pharmaceutical analysis method based on the consideration of drug’s property and pharmacological action and the controllable design of fluorescence probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots with antioxidant activity
Drug interaction
Enoxaparin
Fluorescence strategy
Blood drug concentration monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Real detection of Enox in plasma and drug
Characterization, optical property and antioxidant activity of A-CDs
Feasibility and mechanism of detecting Enox using A-CDs as probes and PMB as medium

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and the size distribution (insert) of A-CDs (A), FTIR spectrum (B) of A-CDs. Fluorescence emission spectra of A-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (C). The UV–vis spectrum (black) of A-CDs and the optimal excitation spectrum (green) and emission spectrum (red) (D) (Insert: The picture of A-CDs under white (left) and ultraviolet lamp (right)). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XPS survey (A), high-resolution XPS surveys of C1s (B), O1s (C), N1s (D) of A-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of A-CDs with different CEnox and PMB (A) (Inset: QE of A-CDs after adding PMB, Enox and PMB-Enox respectively (a), the picture of A-CDs (left) and A-CDs-PMB-Enox (right) in the sunlight (upper) and ultraviolet (lower) (b)). Fluorescence lifetime curves of A-CDs (black), A-CDs-Enox (green), A-CDs-PMB (blue), A-CDs-PMB-Enox (red) (B). The exciting and emission fluorescence spectra of A-CDs and the UV absorption spectra of A-CDs, A-CDs-PMB, A-CDs-Enox, PMB-Enox, A-CDs-PMB-Enox (C). UV absorption spectra of A-CDs-PMB-Enox system at different CEnox (D). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The QEObserved (black) of A-CDs-PMB-Enox and the QECorrected (red) after eliminating the effect of IFE (A). F0 /F value of the reaction system at different temperatures (B). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FTIR spectra of PMB, Enox and PMB-Enox (A), and the FTIR spectra of A-CDs, PMB-Enox, A-CDs-PMB-Enox (B).

The effect of different interfering substances on the QE of A-CDs with the presence of PMB (A) and PMB-Enox (B). (A. PMB-Enox, B. Glucose, C. Fructose, D. Sucrose, E. Lactose, F. KCl, G. NaCl, H. MgCl2 ·6H2 O, I. Na2 CO3 ·10H2 O, J. CaCl2 , K. L-arginine, L. L-histidine, M. L-Glutamate, N. Glutathione, O. L-ascorbic acid, P. Cefoperazone and Sulbactam, Q. Ambroxol, R. Isepamicin, S. Esomeprazole, T. Vancomycin, U. Hyaluronic acid, V. Chondroitin sulfate, W. Compound Amino Acid Injection (18AA-Ⅱ), X·H2 O2 (A: 1 g/mL-100 mg/mL, 100 mg/mL, B-O: 50 mmol/L, P-S: 10 mg/mL, T-W: 1 mg/mL, X: 100 μmol/L.)). Fluorescence emission spectra of A-CDs-PMB-Enox with different CEnox (C). the linear relationship with the QE and CEnox and in the range of 0.8–25.0 μg/mL (D) (Inset: the relation of QE and CEnox). Error bar represented the SD of 3 tests.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of A-CDs and the detection of Enox using PMB as medium.
Determination of recovery rate of spiked Enox in human plasma (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Effectively detect enoxaparin using polymyxin B as medium through fluorescent strategy based on carbon dots with antioxidant activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Fujian Provincial Geriatric Hospital, Teaching Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Teaching Hospital of Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Fujian Provincial Geriatric Hospital, Teaching Hospital of Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Gastric Surgery, Fujian Medical University Union Hospital, Fuzhou 350001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Gastric Surgery, Fujian Medical University Union Hospital, Fuzhou 350001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots with antioxidant activity (A-CDs) were effectively prepared for the simple and accurate monitoring of enoxaparin based on the electrostatic interaction of polymyxin B and enoxaparin with the new formed absorbance peak at 340 nm.

Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) plays an important role in the prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolism. Sensitive and facile monitoring of LMWH is of great significance for clinical special populations. Herein, using enoxaparin (Enox) as the typical representative of LMWH, a convenient and accurate fluorescence method was developed for Enox detection using carbon dots with antioxidant activity (A-CDs) as probe and polymyxin B (PMB) as medium agent. Compared with the simple Enox or PMB, the formed complex of PMB and Enox (PMB-Enox) based on the electrostatic interaction exhibited a new absorbance peak at 340 nm. Correspondingly, the absorbance of PMB-Enox overlapped with the exciting wavelength of A-CDs at 350 nm and effectively quenched the fluorescence of A-CDs through inner filter effect (IFE). The decreased fluorescence intensities of A-CDs were controlled by the increased Enox with constant PMB. A linear fitting equation was obtained between the quenching efficiency (QE) of A-CDs and variable contents of Enox in the range of 0.8–25.0 μg/mL. And the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.64 μg/mL. The recovery of the injection of Enox in human plasma was acceptable with rational relative standard deviations. Moreover, this fluorescence sensing strategy possesses the prospect of developing reliable pharmaceutical analysis method based on the consideration of drug’s property and pharmacological action and the controllable design of fluorescence probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Low-molecular weight heparin (LMWH) is recommended as a first-line treatment drug for prevention and treatment of thrombosis in current clinical guidelines [1]. Depending on the increasing risk of thrombosis, LMWH may be used for continuous several weeks [2]. In addition, the nonanticoagulant properties of LMWH, such as growth factor modification [3,4], anticancer and anti-metastatic properties [5–7], anti-inflammatory properties and antiviral activities [2] and so on, that were also increasingly studied and concerned by the treatment of Corona Virus Disease 2019 [8,9]. Enoxaparin (Enox) is an important kind of LMWH that has been commonly used as an injectable anticoagulant for the prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolism (VTE) [10]. Its anticoagulant effect is mainly achieved by inhibiting the activity of coagulation factors IIa and Xa [2,11,12]. While, bleeding is a common and serious adverse effect of unfractionated heparin (UFH). For treating VTE, Enox has significant advantages in reducing the incidence of bleeding [13]. Due to the predictable pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of Enox, it is not necessary to regularly monitor anti-Xa factor activity in general patients. However, the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of Enox in certain populations may not be predictable, including kidney injury [14–16], abnormal weight [17–19], malignant tumors or pregnancy [20–22], it is meaningful to implement Enox blood drug concentration monitoring for such patients.

The gold standard for evaluating the efficacy of LMWH is based on the assay of anti-Xa factor activity [23]. To date, some techniques have been proposed for the determination of anti-Xa factor activity [23–26], however, these technologies are limited in the detection of heparin and LMWH due to the complex operation or high instrument costs. Benefiting from its sensitivity, detection limit and real-time determination, fluorescence strategies with different sensing principles are extensively developed in pharmaceutical analysis [27,28]. Recently, some fluorescence strategies have been developed for the detection of anticoagulant. With the aggregation-induced emission character, a distyryl-anthracene derivative with four carboxyl groups as fluorescent probe for detecting heparin has been developed with considerable performance [29]. The organic probes prepared from Rhodamine B and fluorene-pyridine are used to detect heparin through fluorescence approach [30,31]. Correspondingly, the interacting system of probe and Fe3+ can be applied to detecting Enox effectively through fluorescence approach [32], prefiguring the prospect of fluorescent strategy for monitoring Enox. While, the reliable fluorescence method with enhanced performance and controllable operation is required by the increasing attentions of monitoring LMWH [12].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of emerging nanomaterials that have attracted widespread attention in the biomedical, food safety, environmental science field due to their biocompatibility, stability, fluorescence, facile synthesis, and controllable surface modification [33–36]. In recent years, analytical methods based on CDs as probe have been successfully applied in the fields of food safety [35,37,38], pathogenic bacteria and viral [39], disease [40], DNA [41] and bioimaging [42]. In addition, different CDs from variable precursors and preparing strategies have different structure and emission wavelengths [36,38], and may interact with specific drugs for drug analysis [43–45]. The existing applications of CDs for drug analysis prefigure the promising application prospects of some CDs in drug detection, especially for LMWH detection. While, there is currently few fluorescent methods for LMWH using CDs as probe, which may be related to the difficulty of directly interacting between CDs and LMWH.

Considering with the potential of the interaction of CDs and drugs, and the necessary of developing simple testing strategy for the monitoring of Enox, in this work, blue fluorescence carbon dots with antioxidant activity (A-CDs) and excellent quantum yield were effectively prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA), L-arginine (L-Arg), and ethylenediamine (EDA) as raw materials (Scheme 1 ). Surprisingly, the prepared A-CDs possessed strong and stable antioxidant activity. The negatively charged Enox can interact with positively charged Polymyxin B (PMB) to be a conjugation (PMB-Enox). Compared with the little effect of Enox or PMB to A-CDs, the formed PMB-Enox effectively quenched the fluorescence intensities of A-CDs. The quenching degree was controlled by the variable Enox with the stable PMB, suggesting the feasibility of the determination of Enox with sensitivity and simple operation (Scheme 1). The accuracy of this strategy verified by the detection of Enox concentration in real samples indicates the promising potential of this method in clinical monitoring of Enox in plasma.

2,2′-Bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diazanium salt (ABTS), Enoxaparin sodium, PMB, CA, L-Arg, and EDA were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Enoxaparin sodium injection (0.4 mL: 4000 AXaIU) (40 mg) was obtained from Sanofi (Beijing) Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. All reagents with analytical grade were used without any purification.

Fluorescence spectra and decay curves were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) and a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain), respectively. Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was collected using a NICOLET iS50 Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Mixed 1.0 g CA and 0.5 g L-Arg in 19 mL ultrapure water in a round-bottom flask. After adding 1 mL EDA, the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor. After heating at 180 ℃ for 6 h, the product was cooled and filtered with 0.22 μM filter membrane. The filtrate was further purified by dialysis using 500–1000 kDa dialysis bag for 24 h. After freeze-drying, a stock solution of A-CDs (2 mg/mL) was prepared and stored at 4 ℃.

The 20 μL A-CDs (0.2 mg/mL) and 20 μL PMB (2 mg/mL) were mixed into 0.5 mL EP tube. Then, the mixture was diluted to 200 μL using PBS (pH 6.5) to be A-CDs-PMB. The diluted mixture solution was centrifuged for 1 min and placed in a constant temperature metal bath at 37 ℃ for 10 min as the control groups. The 20 μL A-CDs (0.2 mg/mL) and 20 μL PMB (2 mg/mL) were respectively mixed with various concentrations of Enox (CEnox) (PMB-Enox). Then, the mixture was diluted to 200 μL using PBS (pH 6.5) to be A-CDs-PMB-Enox. The experimental conditions of the experimental groups (with Enox) were the same as those of the control groups (without Enox). The fluorescence emission spectra of the two groups were recorded in the 360–600 nm wavelength with exciting at 350 nm. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (QE, (F0-F)/F0) was applied as a quantitative standard (F and F0 represent the peak fluorescence intensities of A-CDs-PMB in the presence and absence of Enox, respectively).

Glucose, L-histidine, glutathione, cefoperazone and sulbactam, Ambroxol, Esomeprazole, Vancomycin, Isepamicin and other interferences were mixed with 20 μL A-CDs (0.2 mg/mL) and 20 μL A-CDs-PMB-Enox system (0.2 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL and 1 mg/mL), respectively. The testing process of each interference was the same as the detection procedure of Enox.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real detection of Enox in plasma and drug
ce:section.ce:para: With approval from the Ethics Committee of Fujian Medical University Union Hospital (2022YF041-01), human plasma samples were obtained from healthy volunteers with informed consent form. The 20 μL of 0.2 mg/mL A-CDs, 20 μL of 10 mg/mL PMB and 20 μL of 10 % plasma were added into 0.5 mL EP tube. The mixture was added separately with various concentrations of Enox. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under the same procedure in 2.3 for evaluating the spike recovery.

A certain dose of Enoxaparin sodium injection was diluted with physiological saline to the certain ratio. Then, the content of Enox in the samples was determined by this strategy under the optimal conditions, and calculated the label amount.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization, optical property and antioxidant activity of A-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of A-CDs was initially characterized. As shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 A), A-CDs were uniformly dispersed and circular in shape, with a particle size distribution ranging from 1.3 to 6.7 nm and an average diameter of 3.35 nm. Furthermore, the structural composition of A-CDs was investigated using FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1B), the absorption peaks at 3730 cm−1 and 3620 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of –NH2 and –OH, respectively. The typical absorption bands at 1650 cm−1 and 1540 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO/C-H and CC, respectively, and the absorption peaks at 1370 cm−1 and 797 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C-O-C/C-C, respectively [46,47]. The XPS analysis of A-CDs (Fig. 2 A) revealed three main typical bands attributing to C1s, O1s, and N1s, with the atomic contents of 71.99 %, 22.46 %, and 5.55 %, respectively. The two fitting bands of the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2B) were attributed to C-O/C-N at 286.4 eV and C-C/CC at 284.75 eV, respectively. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2C) were derived to two fitting bands of CO (529.6 eV) and C-OH (531 eV). Similarly, the high-resolution N1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2D) was decomposed into two components of –NH2 (399.1 eV) and Pyrrolic N/Amide N (400.45 eV) [46–48]. The consistent results of FTIR and XPS indicated that the surface of A-CDs was rich in functional groups of –OH, –COOH and –NH2 accompanied with doping effect of N.

The optical properties of A-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1C, A-CDs exhibited excitation-independent luminescence behavior within the excitation wavelength range of 300–400 nm. Correspondingly, the maximum fluorescence emission peak was observed at 440 nm with the excitation wavelength at 350 nm. A-CDs exhibited the main UV absorption peaks centered at 239 nm and 352 nm (Fig. 1D), which can be attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC bond and n-π* electronic transitions of CO group [48]. The inset illustrated that the solution of A-CDs appeared colorless and transparent under sunlight but exhibited a strong blue light when exposed to ultraviolet light.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of A-CDs was evaluated using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference. As shown in Fig. S1A, the relative QY of A-CDs was calculated to be 15.9 % based on Equation S1, suggesting the excellent luminescence performance of A-CDs. The fluorescence stability of A-CDs was assessed. As shown in Fig. S1B, the fluorescence intensities of A-CDs remained above 90.9 % across a pH range of 4–11. However, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensities was observed under strongly acidic (pH 3) and basic (pH 12) conditions, indicating that the fluorescence was considerably weakened by high acid and basic concentration. As shown in Fig. S1C, A-CDs retained over 94.4 % fluorescence intensities across a temperature range of 4 to 75 ℃, indicating the little sensitivity of A-CDs to temperature variations. Additionally, as depicted in Fig. S1D, A-CDs maintained over 96.7 % fluorescence intensities in salt solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 2000 mM, indicating the stability of A-CDs even in high salt environment. When continuously excited at 350 nm for 1 h (Fig. S1E) and stored at 4 ℃ for 180 days (Fig. S1F), the fluorescence intensities of A-CDs fluctuated slightly, indicating excellent anti-photobleaching and long-term fluorescence stability.

ABTS•+ radical cation analysis is considered as one of the most effective methods for assessing antioxidant activity [45,49]. The antioxidant activity of A-CDs was evaluated using ABTS•+. As shown in Fig. S2A, increased A-CDs led to a gradual decrease of the absorbance of the ABTS•+ solution at the characteristic absorption peak of 728 nm. Concurrently, the solution color transitioned from blue to light yellow (Fig. S2A). With rising concentrations of A-CDs and ascorbic acid (AA), the clearance efficiencies of both A-CDs and AA on the ABTS•+ solution increased (Fig. S2B). The half maximum inhibitory concentrations (EC50) of A-CDs and AA on ABTS•+ solution were determined to be 4.87 μg/mL and 4.07 μg/mL, respectively. These results indicated that A-CDs exhibited significant scavenging activity against ABTS•+, implying the strong antioxidant activity of A-CDs. The antioxidant activity of A-CDs may be due to the functional groups of –NH2 and –OH of A-CDs. The results indirectly confirmed the characterization of the chemical information of A-CDs by FTIR and XPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism of detecting Enox using A-CDs as probes and PMB as medium
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 A, neither PMB nor Enox alone affected the fluorescence intensities of A-CDs (the upper inset of Fig. 3A). However, the combination of PMB and Enox (PMB-Enox) resulted in a notable decrease in the fluorescence signal of A-CDs. As CEnox increased, the fluorescence intensities of A-CDs gradually weakened. The appearance of the detecting solution changed from colorless and transparent to light white flocculent under sunlight, with the gradually weakened blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (the lower inset of Fig. 3A). The findings confirm that it was feasible to construct a fluorescence strategy with A-CDs as fluorescence probe and PMB as medium for detecting Enox.

The detecting mechanism was investigated. The fluorescence lifetime curves (Fig. 3B) of the four components of A-CDs, A-CDs-Enox, A-CDs-PMB, A-CDs-PMB-Enox were nearly overlapped. And the fluorescence lifetimes were measured to be 15.09 ns, 14.96 ns, 14.90 ns and 15.24 ns, respectively (Table S1). The maximal variation in lifetime was only 0.34 ns, suggesting that the quenching of A-CDs by the formed PMB-Enox was not primarily due to dynamic quenching effects (DQE) [44].

The emission spectrum of A-CDs showed no overlap with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of PMB-Enox (Fig. 3C). It indicated that the quenching of A-CDs by PMB-Enox was not due to fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET) [44]. In addition, It showed that PMB-Enox generated a new substance with the new formed absorption peak at 340 nm, while Enox or PMB alone did not exhibit UV absorption at 340 nm, which coincided with the exciting wavelength of A-CDs at 350 nm (Fig. 1D). A certain degree of overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the absorbing substance and the excitation and/or emission spectra of probe can induce the happen of an inner filter effect (IFE) [50]. The interaction of A-CDs and PMB-Enox was further evaluated. As CEnox gradually increased, the shoulder absorbance peak at 340 nm of A-CDs-PMB-Enox gradually increased (Fig. 3D), preliminarily inferring the weak interaction of A-CDs and PMB-Enox.

The absorbance and fluorescence intensities of A-CDs-PMB with different CEnox were measured to determine the visible fluorescence quenching efficiency (QEObserved). Based on the geometric shape of the quartz cuvette (Fig. S3), the corrected fluorescence quenching efficiency (QECorrected) with the elimination of IFE was calculated (Equation S2) [46,50,51]. The results of QEObserved and QECorrected with different CEnox were summarized in Table S2 and Fig. 4 A. The QEObserved and calculated QECorrected by Equation S2 were significantly different. The inhibitory efficiency (SE) of IFE on PMB-Enox to A-CDs reached 73.95 % (Equation S3). Therefore, it can be concluded that the testing strategy of Enox with A-CDs as probe and PMB as medium was caused by formed PMB-Enox through IFE. Ascribed to the kept fluorescence lifetime of A-CDs in the testing process, the potential static quenching effect (SQE) was also evaluated. The Sterm-Volmer equation was obtained through the testing of quenching constant at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence quenching constant (K) reduced from 0.01923 mL/μg to 0.01393 mL/μg as the temperature increased from 25 ℃ to 50 ℃, indicating the presence of SQE for the quenched A-CDs [44,51].

In the Enox detection system, we divided them into two groups and compared the changes of FTIR spectra before and after the reaction. The absorption peak at 3494 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of PMB should belong to the –OH expansion vibration of the terminal amino group of PMB (Fig. 5 A), the absorption peak at 3282 cm−1 in the spectrum should belong to the –NH2 expansion vibration, and the absorption peak at 1119 cm−1 belonged to the expansion vibration of C-N connected by the amino group and carbon [43,45]. Compared to the FTIR spectrum of PMB, PMB-Enox showed a significant increase in the FTIR absorption peak at 3454 cm−1 (Fig. 5A), likely due to the overlap of –OH from Enox and –NH2 from PMB. In contrast, the absorption peak of C-N and CO of PMB at 1119 cm−1 and 1537 cm−1 were significantly weakened after adding Enox, suggesting that these two groups on PMB reacted with Enox, resulting in altered absorption. This phenomenon is closely related to the principle of micellized conjugated system [52]. In the FTIR spectrum of A-CDs (Fig. 5B), the absorption peak at 3677 cm−1 was attributed to the –OH stretching vibration. The typical absorption bands at 2920 cm−1 can be considered as C-H stretching vibration, while the absorption peak at 1540 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching of CC [43,45]. Additionally, the absorption of CC of A-CDs at 1540 cm−1 was weakened after adding PMB-Enox (Fig. 5B). While, no new functional groups were generated. Their functional groups were changed after mixing A-CDs, PMB and Enox, suggesting the formed non-fluorescence compound, which was consistent with the mechanism of SQE. In summary, the quenching of A-CDs by the addition of Enox with the presence of PMB was resulted from the combined effects of IFE and SQE.

Using QE as the evaluative parameter, several operation conditions were optimized. Compared to the buffer solutions of Tris-HCl and HAc-NaAc, the A-CDs-PMB-Enox system exhibited the highest QE in PBS (Fig. S4A). The QE of detection system varied with pH in PBS, accompanying with the maximum QE at pH 6.5 (Fig. S4B). Although the QE was highest at 4 ℃ (Fig. S4C), considering the potential application of this research method in physiological environments, 37 ℃ was chosen as the reaction temperature. The fluorescence response varied with different reaction times (Fig. S4D). And a reaction time of 2 min was determined to be optimal. As the concentration of PMB (CPMB) increased, the QE showed a trend of initial increasing and then decreasing (Fig. S4E). Correspondingly, CPMB at 1000 μg/mL was selected as the optimal concentration for the detection.

Patients who require anticoagulant therapy often suffer from conditions such as atrial fibrillation (AF) and VTE, particularly among the elderly and frail patients. These patients may experience various pathological and physiological changes, leading to diverse medication combinations [53]. Considering the clinical applicability of this detection strategy, the possible effects of commonly used drugs, common physiological substances and heparin analogues in clinical elderly patients were applied to evaluate the specificity of this strategy. As shown in Fig. 6 A, with the presence of PMB, only the addition of Enox exhibited a significant quenching effect on A-CDs, all of the interfering substances had little response on the fluorescence intensities of A-CDs. In the detection system featuring both PMB and Enox (Fig. 6B), the effects of the coexistence of interference on the QE were also negligible. Moreover, the potential effects of metal ions on the testing system were also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S5, except the slight response of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on both of the systems of A-CDs and A-CDs-PMB with the absence and presence of Enox, other metal ions exhibited almost no interference on the fluorescence responses of the testing system. These results indicated the high selectivity and specificity of this strategy under both physiological and pathological conditions for detecting Enox.

Fluorescence emission spectra of A-CDs-PMB with different CEnox were scanned under the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 6C, with the increasing of CEnox, the fluorescence intensities of the system gradually decreased, suggesting the gradually increased QE. Fig. 6D showed a good linear relationship between QE and CEnox in the range of 0.8–25.0 μg/mL, which can be fitted to the linear equation of y = 0.0115 CEnox + 0.01034 with R2 = 0.9937. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.64 μg/mL according to 3σ/S (where S and σ were the slope of the linear equation and standard deviation (SD) of 13 blank determinations, respectively). The parallel results of 10 samples of Enox at 2 μg/mL were tested and compared to investigate the reproducibility. The process was applied to measure two samples daily for five consecutive days. As shown in Fig. S6, the QEs of the 10 measurements were close to each other with the SD and RSD of 0.0072 and 9.90 %, respectively. In addition, several analytical methods for Enox were compared and listed in Table S3. Besides the testing application, this method exhibited acceptable performance in terms of linear range and LOD. Compared with other methods, this method exhibited the characteristics of rapidness and convenience, suggesting the promising prospect of the strategy in monitoring Enox.

The initial utility of this method was evaluated for the detection of Enox in human plasma. As shown in Table 1 , the spike recovery of variable concentrations of Enox in human plasma ranged from 94.43 % to 108.56 %, with RSDs of 2.10–7.91 %, indicating the potential of this method for monitoring clinical plasma concentrations. Furthermore, the strategy was applied to detect the content of Enox in actual injection samples. The detection was carried out through the addition of different dilutions of the injection. The labeled amount of Enox of the injection was 101.9 %, 102.6 %, and 106.0 % with three dilution factors. And the RSDs were less than 1 %, indicating that this strategy could be applied for the determination of CEnox for quality control of drug according to the Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020.

In summary, A-CDs emitting blue fluorescence were fabricated and applied as probes to conveniently and accurately detect Enox using PMB as medium agent based on the dual effect of IFE and SQE. the specific interaction of Enox and PMB based on the structure characteristic of each other exhibited the critical role in this strategy. This method was successfully used for the detection of Enox in plasma and Enoxaparin content in injection. This fluorescence sensing strategy had high reproducibility and reliability and had promising applications in implementing Enox blood drug concentration monitoring.

Liang Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rouying Cai: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yao Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Chenfang Miao: Methodology, Formal analysis. Menghan Zhang: Software, Methodology. Chen Zheng: Supervision, Project administration. Jianzhong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhengjun Huang: Resources. Shaohuang Weng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was finically supported by the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2020J01999, 2022J01493), and the Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2021Y9007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112615.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
